---
title: "Warping maleCNS DA1 projection neurons onto the BANC"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Warping maleCNS DA1 projection neurons onto the BANC}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>", eval = FALSE, purl = FALSE)
# The data-pull and Deformetrica chunks need neuPrint/CAVE access tokens and a
# working Deformetrica (>= 4.3) install, so they are eval = FALSE on CI. They run
# end-to-end locally on a CPU once tokens and Deformetrica are set up (see setup).
```

## Goal

Take a cell type reconstructed in **two different adult *Drosophila* connectomes** —
the isolated-CNS EM volume **maleCNS** and the whole central-nervous-system
**BANC** — and warp one onto the other so the same neurons can be compared in a
single coordinate frame. We use the **DA1 antennal-lobe projection neurons**
(`DA1_lPN` + `DA1_vPN`), a small, well-defined, bilaterally paired population.

Two brains, two segmentations, two template spaces: a *template bridge* gets the
maleCNS neurons roughly into BANC space, and then a **Deformetrica diffeomorphism
fit to the projection neurons themselves** does the fine, local alignment — matched
**left-to-left and right-to-right** so a warp can never fold one hemisphere's
neurons onto the other's.

![maleCNS DA1 projection neurons (red) warping onto their BANC counterparts (blue), inside a translucent BANC brain-neuropil hull; left and right matched separately.](../man/figures/malecns_to_banc.gif)

The recipe is the general cross-dataset one:

1. a **template bridge** maleCNS → FAFB14 → JRC2018F → BANC (`xform_brain()` +
   `bancr::banc_to_JRC2018F()`), which lands the maleCNS neurons on top of the BANC
   ones to within a few microns, then
2. a **multi-object diffeomorphism** (`deformetrica_register_multi()`) fit to the
   left and right projection-neuron sets at once — each side an all-to-all
   `Varifold` match, no neuron-to-neuron correspondence needed — plus an **ordered
   soma point-correspondence** (a `Landmark` object) that maps the cell-body clusters
   onto one another, regularised by the BANC brain-neuropil hull, and
3. a **nat.ggplot animation** of the geodesic flow (`ggplot_flow_gif()`).

Aligning the **somata** matters: a soma is a single point at the end of a
backbone, so it carries almost no `Varifold` curve-mass — the arbour match will
happily leave the cell bodies tens of microns apart. An explicit soma landmark
fixes that, and the soma positions are already curated in both datasets (the
maleCNS neuPrint `somaLocation`, the BANC nucleus table).

```{r setup}
library(deformetricar)
library(nat)
library(malecns)        # maleCNS (male-cns:v0.9) neuprint client + bridging landmarks
library(bancr)          # BANC CAVE/neuprint client + JRC2018F<->BANC bridge + surfaces
library(neuprintr)
library(nat.flybrains); library(nat.jrcbrains); library(nat.templatebrains)
library(fafbseg)
library(Rvcg)           # mesh decimation

# Tokens: the maleCNS and BANC data live behind neuPrint / CAVE. Follow the setup in
# the malecns, bancr and neuprintr READMEs to get your own tokens into ~/.Renviron
# before running this vignette.
#
# One-time: set up the Deformetrica CLI this package wraps, in a managed conda env.
# (Skip if find_deformetrica() already resolves, e.g. options(deformetricar.exe=).)
# install_deformetrica()
```

Everything below runs locally on a CPU (`device = "cpu"`); `device = "auto"` uses a
CUDA GPU if one is present. The projection-neuron fit takes a few minutes on a CPU
(much faster on a GPU).

## 1. The DA1 projection neurons in each dataset

Both connectomes annotate the DA1 uniglomerular projection neurons as `DA1_lPN`
and `DA1_vPN`; we exclude the DA1 olfactory receptor neurons (`ORN_DA1`). Each
neuron carries a soma side, which we keep so the two hemispheres stay separate.

```{r fetch}
## maleCNS: metadata + skeletons (native maleCNS nm space), resampled to ~1 µm.
mm <- mcns_neuprint_meta("DA1")
mm <- mm[mm$type %in% c("DA1_lPN", "DA1_vPN"), ]         # 15 PNs (8 L / 7 R)
mn <- read_mcns_neurons(mm$bodyid)
mn <- nlapply(mn, resample, stepsize = 1000)
m_side <- setNames(as.character(mm$somaSide), as.character(mm$bodyid))[names(mn)]

## BANC: metadata + L2 skeletons (native BANC nm space), resampled to ~1 µm.
bm <- banc_meta()
bm <- bm[bm$type %in% c("DA1_lPN", "DA1_vPN"), ]          # 17 PNs (8 left / 9 right)
bn <- banc_read_l2skel(bm$id)
bn <- nlapply(bn, resample, stepsize = 1000)
b_side <- setNames(as.character(bm$side), as.character(bm$id))[names(bn)]

## Soma positions. maleCNS: the neuPrint `somaLocation` is an "x,y,z" string in
## 8 nm voxels, so multiply by 8 for native nm. BANC: the curated nucleus table
## (`banc_nuclei()`) gives `nucleus_position_nm` per neuron.
ms_native <- do.call(rbind, lapply(strsplit(mm$somaLocation, ","), as.numeric)) * 8
msoma <- data.frame(ms_native, side = mm$somaSide, type = mm$type)
names(msoma)[1:3] <- c("X", "Y", "Z")

nuc   <- banc_nuclei(rootids = bm$id)                     # nucleus_position_nm (nm)
bsoma <- data.frame(xyzmatrix(nuc$nucleus_position_nm),
                    root_id = as.character(nuc$root_id))
names(bsoma)[1:3] <- c("X", "Y", "Z")
bsoma <- bsoma[!is.na(bsoma$X) & !duplicated(bsoma$root_id), ]
bsoma <- cbind(bsoma, bm[match(bsoma$root_id, as.character(bm$id)), c("side", "type")])
bsoma$side <- ifelse(bsoma$side == "left", "L", ifelse(bsoma$side == "right", "R", bsoma$side))
bsoma <- bsoma[!is.na(bsoma$type), ]
```

## 2. Bridge the maleCNS neurons into BANC space

maleCNS and BANC each have their own template. We hop through the shared fly
templates: the `malecns` package ships landmark bridges to **FAFB14**, `nat.jrcbrains`
carries **FAFB14 → JRC2018F**, and `bancr` inverts its own **JRC2018F ↔ BANC**
thin-plate spline. The result puts each maleCNS neuron within a few microns of its
BANC homologue — a good starting point for the diffeomorphism to refine.

```{r bridge}
# Register the malecns <-> FAFB14 landmark bridges (bundled in the malecns package).
malecns:::mcns_register_xforms()

# The same three hops carry the neurons AND the maleCNS somata into BANC space.
to_banc <- function(x) {
  x <- xform_brain(x, sample = "malecns", reference = "FAFB14")
  x <- xform_brain(x, sample = "FAFB14",  reference = "JRC2018F")
  banc_to_JRC2018F(x, region = "brain", banc.units = "nm", inverse = TRUE)   # -> BANC nm
}
mn <- to_banc(mn)
xyzmatrix(msoma) <- to_banc(xyzmatrix(msoma))

# Deformetrica needs O(1-100) coordinates, so work in microns from here on.
um <- function(x) { xyzmatrix(x) <- xyzmatrix(x) / 1000; x }
mn <- um(mn); bn <- um(bn); msoma <- um(msoma); bsoma <- um(bsoma)
```

## 3. Split per side, and build the soma correspondence

We register the neurons **one object per hemisphere**: all of one side's maleCNS
PNs against all of that side's BANC PNs. Because there is no neuron-to-neuron
correspondence across datasets, each side is matched as an unlabelled bundle of
curves (a `Varifold` attachment); keeping the sides as separate objects is what
stops the fit swapping a left neuron onto a right one.

```{r split}
mn_L <- mn[m_side == "L"];    mn_R <- mn[m_side == "R"]
bn_L <- bn[b_side == "left"]; bn_R <- bn[b_side == "right"]
```

For the somata we *can* build an ordered correspondence: collapse each dataset's
cell bodies to one centroid **per (side, type)** group, and match the groups the two
datasets share (`L DA1_lPN`, `R DA1_lPN`, `L DA1_vPN`, `R DA1_vPN`). That gives a
handful of matched points — enough to pin the lateral (`lPN`) and ventral (`vPN`)
cell-body clusters of each hemisphere onto their BANC counterparts.

```{r soma-corr}
centroids <- function(df) {
  grp <- paste(df$side, df$type)
  do.call(rbind, lapply(split(df, grp), function(s)
    data.frame(grp = paste(s$side[1], s$type[1]),
               t(colMeans(xyzmatrix(s))))))
}
mc <- centroids(msoma); bc <- centroids(bsoma)
common   <- intersect(mc$grp, bc$grp)
soma_src <- as.matrix(mc[match(common, mc$grp), c("X", "Y", "Z")])
soma_tgt <- as.matrix(bc[match(common, bc$grp), c("X", "Y", "Z")])
rownames(soma_src) <- rownames(soma_tgt) <- common
```

Finally, a sparse point cloud sampled from the BANC brain-neuropil hull is used as an
identity anchor (same points as source and target): it pins the far field
near-identity so the warp stays local to the antennal-lobe / projection-neuron
region. A sparse `Landmark` object is much cheaper than a full mesh `Current`
attachment for the same regularising role.

```{r hull-anchor}
hull <- um(as.mesh3d(bancr::banc_brain_neuropil.surf))
set.seed(1)
hv  <- xyzmatrix(hull)
anc <- hv[sample(nrow(hv), 400L), ]
```

## 4. One diffeomorphism for both sides

`deformetrica_register_multi()` fits a single diffeomorphism to the left PNs, the
right PNs and the **soma correspondence** at once, anchored by the sparse hull point
cloud. `data_sigma` weights each object (*smaller = stronger*): the projection
neurons drive the fit; the somata (an ordered `Landmark` object — a plain matrix is
matched point-to-point) get a firm pull so the cell bodies land on target; the hull
anchor (passed via `landmarks`) is only a weak envelope (`landmark_sigma`).
`object_kernel_width` sets the scale at which each object's mismatch is measured —
tight for the neurons (local, tens of microns). The global `kernel_width`
(deformation stiffness) is set from the brain bounding box.

```{r fit}
# Coarsen the skeletons to ~3 µm for the fit: the Varifold attachment cost scales with
# point count, and 3 µm is plenty for aligning arbours. (We still shoot the full-detail
# neurons for the animation below.)
co   <- function(nl) nlapply(nl, resample, stepsize = 3)
srcs <- list(PN_L = co(mn_L), PN_R = co(mn_R), soma = soma_src)
tgts <- list(PN_L = co(bn_L), PN_R = co(bn_R), soma = soma_tgt)

V  <- xyzmatrix(hull)
kw <- sqrt(sum((apply(V, 2, max) - apply(V, 2, min))^2)) / 15    # ~57 µm
fit <- deformetrica_register_multi(
  srcs, tgts, kernel_width = kw,
  object_kernel_width = c(PN_L = 15, PN_R = 15, soma = kw),
  data_sigma          = c(PN_L = 2,  PN_R = 2,  soma = 3),
  landmarks = list(source = anc, target = anc), landmark_sigma = 8,
  timepoints = 15L, max_iterations = 40L, device = "cpu")
```

## 5. Animate the warp, coloured by dataset

`deformetrica_shoot(..., flow = TRUE)` returns every timepoint of the geodesic
flow; `ggplot_flow_gif()` renders the maleCNS neurons (red) flowing onto the static
BANC targets (blue) inside the translucent brain hull. We use BANC's canonical
frontal view.

```{r animate}
flowL <- deformetrica_shoot(mn_L, fit$control_points, fit$momenta,
           kernel_width = fit$kernel_width, timepoints = 15L, device = "cpu", flow = TRUE)
flowR <- deformetrica_shoot(mn_R, fit$control_points, fit$momenta,
           kernel_width = fit$kernel_width, timepoints = 15L, device = "cpu", flow = TRUE)

ggplot_flow_gif(
  list(maleCNS_L = flowL, maleCNS_R = flowR),
  cols = c(maleCNS_L = "#EE4266", maleCNS_R = "#EE4266"), alpha = 0.9,
  volume = hull, volume_col = "grey70", volume_alpha = 0.06,
  targets = list(BANC_L = bn_L, BANC_R = bn_R),
  target_cols = c(BANC_L = "#2C7FB8", BANC_R = "#2C7FB8"), target_alpha = 0.55,
  rotation_matrix = bancr::banc_rotation_matrices[["front"]], delay = 0.15,
  file = "malecns_to_banc.gif")   # needs the gifski (or magick) package
```

## 6. Validation — does the warp tighten the cross-dataset match?

With no neuron-to-neuron correspondence, we score the match by the **median
nearest-point distance** from each warped maleCNS neuron point to the nearest BANC
neuron point, per side, and check the warp beats the template-bridge baseline.

```{r validation}
nnd <- function(a, b) median(Rvcg::vcgKDtree(xyzmatrix(b), xyzmatrix(a), k = 1)$distance)
warp <- function(x) deformetrica_shoot(x, fit$control_points, fit$momenta,
                                       kernel_width = fit$kernel_width)
data.frame(
  side   = c("L", "R"),
  bridge = c(nnd(mn_L,        bn_L), nnd(mn_R,        bn_R)),
  warp   = c(nnd(warp(mn_L),  bn_L), nnd(warp(mn_R),  bn_R)))   # warp should be smaller

# Soma correspondence: distance from each warped maleCNS centroid to its BANC target.
ws <- xyzmatrix(warp(soma_src))
data.frame(group  = rownames(soma_src),
           bridge = sqrt(rowSums((soma_src - soma_tgt)^2)),
           warp   = sqrt(rowSums((ws       - soma_tgt)^2)))
```

## Notes

- **Why the projection neurons and not the whole brain?** A cross-dataset warp is
  only as good as the structures it is driven by. The DA1 PNs are unambiguous in
  both datasets and span the antennal lobe, lateral horn and calyx, so a fit to them
  aligns the whole olfactory-relevant swathe of the brain — more usefully, here,
  than a fit to the outer hull alone.
- **All-to-all, not one-to-one.** maleCNS has 15 DA1 PNs and BANC 17; there is no
  natural pairing. The `Varifold` attachment matches each side's neurons as an
  unlabelled bundle of curves, so unequal counts are fine.
- **Somata need their own landmark.** The `Varifold` term aligns arbours but barely
  feels a soma (one point, negligible curve-mass), so the template bridge can leave
  cell bodies tens of microns off — here the ventral-PN (`vPN`) clusters start ~40 µm
  apart. Matching soma **centroids per (side, type)** as an ordered `Landmark` object
  pulls them together without needing a full neuron-to-neuron pairing. Use the curated
  soma fields (maleCNS `somaLocation`, BANC `banc_nuclei()`), not the skeleton root,
  which for an L2 skeleton need not sit at the soma.
- **The hull anchor is a regulariser, not a target.** Passing the *same* sampled
  hull points as `landmarks$source` and `landmarks$target` pins the far field
  near-identity, so the diffeomorphism spends its flexibility on the projection
  neurons rather than drifting the rest of the brain. A sparse Landmark point cloud
  does this far more cheaply than a full brain-surface `Current` attachment — pass
  the mesh as its own source/target object if you want it to *deform* too.
- **Tuning levers.** `data_sigma` (attachment weight, smaller = stronger),
  `object_kernel_width` (matching scale, smaller = finer) and the global
  `kernel_width` (deformation stiffness) trade global against local fit — see the
  mosquito-to-fly vignette for the same three levers on a harder, cross-species case.
- **Template bridge.** The maleCNS → FAFB14 → JRC2018F → BANC hop reuses published
  registrations; the residual it leaves (a few microns) is exactly what the
  diffeomorphism is there to remove. Swap in a different target dataset by changing
  only the final `banc_to_JRC2018F()` step.
